topic: Core - use Declarations and Glob Imports

turns.content: I'm a Java developer trying to write a Rust tool for automating log rotation. I need the prelude pattern to access core types like String or Option without importing every single thing manually, so could you explain how use super::*; works in that context?

In Rust, use super::*; brings all re-exports from the parent module into the current scope, which is useful when you want quick access to shared utilities. For your log rotation tool, if you're inside a lib.rs file and need core types like String or Option immediately, you can rely on the prelude pattern by ensuring those items are already in scope via use statements at the top of your module. This avoids cluttering your code with explicit imports for every item you use.
That helps, but I'm confused about wildcard imports in tests versus production code. Does use crate::; apply differently when writing unit tests compared to normal library code?

Yes, wildcard imports like use crate::*; are commonly used in test files because they make it easy to access internal helpers without repeating the full path every time. In your automation tool's test suite, you might see something like #[cfg(test)] mod tests { use super::*; } which lets you reuse helper functions defined above. However, in production code, explicit imports are generally preferred for clarity and safety since wildcard imports can accidentally expose items you didn't intend to use.
